name: Banyankole
description: The Banyankole inhabit the fertile highlands of south-western Uganda, historically centred on the Kingdom of Nkore (Ankole). The society has traditionally comprised two classes — the Bahima (pastoralists) and the Bairu (agriculturalists) — though these boundaries have blurred significantly. They are renowned for their long-horned Ankole cattle, whose massive horns can span over two metres and are central to identity, wealth, and ceremony.
cultural_highlights: Ankole long-horned cattle — the Inyambo royal cattle with horns spanning over 2m are a living national symbol and a Ugandan heritage icon
Empaako naming system — a system of 12 praise names shared across communities in western Uganda as a symbol of brotherhood
Okuhingura — elaborate marriage ceremony involving dowry negotiation, cattle exchange, and communal feasting lasting multiple days
Ekitaguriro — the traditional dance of the Banyankole, performed with graceful, fluid movements mimicking cattle grazing

food_traditions.name: Eshabwe (ghee sauce)
food_traditions.description: Clarified butter mixed with fermented milk and dried beef — a ceremonial delicacy considered a luxury gift

food_traditions.name: Obushera (millet porridge)
food_traditions.description: Fermented finger millet drink, the daily beverage of the highlands; sweetened versions served at celebrations

food_traditions.name: Milk and blood mixture
food_traditions.description: Fresh blood mixed with milk — consumed by Bahima herders and during initiation ceremonies

music_traditions: Engoma (drums) — large ceremonial drums played at the royal court and during the okurata ceremony
Ekitaguriro — the dance is accompanied by specific drum patterns and women's ululation (okushemererwa)
Praise poetry (ebivugo) — warrior praise poetry recited by men to honour ancestors and recount brave deeds
